sanskrit_devanagari:
यत्र काले त्वनावृत्तिमावृत्तिं चैव योगिनः ।
प्रयाता यान्ति तं कालं वक्ष्यामि भरतर्षभ ॥ २३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yatra kāle tv anāvṛttim āvṛttiṃ caiva yoginaḥ
prayātā yānti taṃ kālaṃ vakṣyāmi bharatarṣabha || 23 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; vedabase concatenates the two halves; danda restored from gitasupersite)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।8.23।।)
exact IAST (verbatim from page; sandhi conventions match GRETIL)

translations.verbatim_quote: O best of the Bhāratas, I shall now explain to you the different times at which, passing away from this world, the yogī does or does not come back.
I shall now describe to you the different paths departing by which, during death, the yogis do or do not come back, O best of the Bharatas.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The unalloyed devotees of the Supreme Lord, who are totally surrendered souls, do not care when they leave their bodies or by what method. They leave everything in Kṛṣṇa’s hands and so easily and happily return to Godhead.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Prabhupada glosses BG 8.23 in the Gaudiya Vaishnava key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.23 Bharatarsabha, O best of the Bharata dynasty; vaksyami, I shall speak; tu, now; tam, of that; kalam, time; prayatah, by departing, by dying; (-these words are to be which time; yoginah, the yogis; yanti, attain; anavrttim, the State of Non-return, of nonrebirth; ca eva, and also; of t…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads BG 8.23 in the Advaita non-dual key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.23 – 8.24 Here, the term ‘time’ denotes a path, having many deities beginning with day and ending with year. The deities preside over divisions of time. The meaning is — I declare to you the path departing in which Yogins do not return and also the path departing in which the doers of go…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads BG 8.23 in the Vishishtadvaita devotional-qualified key.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Thus it has been explained by Lord Krishna that the worshippers of the Supreme Lord having successfully attaining Him never return to the material existence, whereas others continue to revolve in samsara the perpetual cycle of birth and death. So the question might be asked, by which path…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Sridhara provides a synthetic gloss bridging Advaita and devotional readings.

positions.rendering: the different times at which, passing away from this world, the yogi does or does not come back
the different paths departing by which the yogis do or do not come back
the time at which yogis attain non-return or its opposite (return); 'yogis' implies both meditators and men of actions
the term 'time' denotes a path with deities presiding over divisions of time

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The verse opens the eschatology of 8.24-8.27. Shankara reads 'time' literally — the time at which one departs determines outcome; 'yogis' includes both meditators and karmins. Ramanuja explicitly reads kāla as denoting a path, with the times of 8.24-8.25 functioning as deities presiding over portions of the path. Modern translations are split on whether kāla means 'time' or 'path.' The engineering layer cannot honor the eschatological content; the surface meaning is preserved.

literal_meaning: At what time, departing, yogis attain non-return or return — that time I shall describe to you, O bull of the Bharatas.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna pivots into the eschatology of departure. 'I shall now describe the time/path at which yogis depart and attain either non-return or return.' Shankara reads kāla as time; the deities of time govern outcome. Ramanuja explicitly reads kāla as denoting a path with deities presiding over portions of time. The verse opens the bright-path/dark-path block (8.23-8.27).

remaining_gaps: Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; bhagavad-gita.us static-HTML excerpt captured but full Adidevananda edition would deepen the citation.
